<<<<<<< HEAD
---
title: " Calculate Raw Score and testing different model for meta analysis"
author: "Prerana Chandratre"
date: "02/07/2025"
output: html_document
---


```{r Load Required Libraries and Import Data}

# Load Required Libraries and Import Data
library(openxlsx)   # Library to read/write Excel files
library(dplyr)      # Library for efficient data manipulation using pipes and verbs
library(tidyr)      # Library for reshaping and tidying data
library(stringr)    # Library for string operations and pattern matching

# Define file paths
file_path <- "data/included_studies_1-13.xlsx"           # Path to the input Excel file (AgeWell dataset)
output_path <- "output/long_format_with_seq_corr.xlsx"   # Placeholder path for output (not used yet)

# Read the Excel file starting from row 2, assuming headers start on the second row
data <- read.xlsx(file_path, startRow = 2)

# Define a function to clean and standardize column names
clean_names <- function(x) {
  x <- gsub('["\']', "", x)          # Remove double and single quotes
  x <- gsub("&#10;?", "", x)         # Remove HTML newline codes (like '&#10;')
  x <- gsub("[\r\n]", "", x)         # Remove actual newline characters (carriage returns and linefeeds)
  x <- trimws(x)                     # Trim leading and trailing whitespace
  x <- gsub("\\s+", "", x)           # Remove all inner spaces
  return(x)                          # Return the cleaned name vector
}

# Apply the cleaning function to all column names of the data
names(data) <- clean_names(names(data))

```


```{r static-and-dynamic-columns}
# Define Static Columns and Filter WB Variables


# List of static columns (subject identifiers, authors, year, etc.)
keep_static <- c("0_IDF", "1_ID", "1.1_CaseID", "2_Author", "3_Year", "29_WbPoint", "10_Size", "11_Mage", "16_CNT", "17_Eth", "18_Female","Negative/Positive+LS", "30_Covid", "31_WBm", "33_Relt1", "34_Relt")

# Regex patterns to match dynamic WB columns
wb_patterns <- c(
  "^\\d+_WBmt\\d+$",           # e.g. 12_WBmt1
  "^\\d+_WBsdt\\d+$",          # e.g. 12_WBsdt1
  "^\\d+_WBt(\\d+)t(\\d+)$",   # e.g. 12_WBt1t2
  "^\\d+_WBcort(\\d+)t(\\d+)$" # e.g. 12_WBcort1t2
)

# Collect columns that match the WB patterns
wb_cols <- unique(unlist(lapply(wb_patterns, function(p) grep(p, names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE))))

# Filter to include only time-sequential intervals (e.g., 1-2, 2-3, ... 11-12)
is_sequential <- function(name) {
  match <- str_match(name, "t(\\d+)t(\\d+)")
  #if (any(is.na(match[1, 2:3]))) return(TRUE) 
  if (is.na(match[1,2]) || is.na(match[1,3])) return(TRUE)  # Allow mt/sdt columns without time ranges
  return(as.integer(match[1,3]) - as.integer(match[1,2]) == 1)  # Ensure intervals are sequential
  #return(as.integer(match[1, 3]) - as.integer(match[1, 2]) == 1)
}
wb_cols <- wb_cols[sapply(wb_cols, is_sequential)]

# Combine static and dynamic columns
all_cols <- intersect(c(keep_static, wb_cols), names(data))

# Subset the data with selected columns
data <- data[, all_cols, drop = FALSE]

# Convert all non-static (i.e., dynamic WB) columns to numeric
data[setdiff(names(data), keep_static)] <- lapply(data[setdiff(names(data), keep_static)], as.numeric)

```

```{r Adding time intervals}
# Define Time Intervals
timepoints <- 1:11                       # From timepoint 1 to 11 (for intervals 1-2 to 11-12)
intervals <- paste0(timepoints, "-", timepoints + 1)  # Create interval labels (e.g., "1-2", "2-3", etc.)

# Prepare an empty list to store reshaped rows
long_rows <- list()

```


```{r Transform Each Row into Long Format}

# Predefine expected WBmt and WBsdt column names
all_wbmt_cols <- paste0("_WBmt", 1:12)
all_wbsdt_cols <- paste0("_WBsdt", 1:12)

# Loop over each case/row
for (i in 1:nrow(data)) {
  static_info <- data[i, keep_static]  # Extract static info for this row

  # Iterate over each time interval (1-2, 2-3, ..., 11-12)
  for (tp in timepoints) {
    interval_label <- paste0(tp, "-", tp + 1)

    # Dynamically get column names for required variables for this interval
    mean1_col       <- grep(paste0("_WBmt", tp, "$"), names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)
    sd1_col         <- grep(paste0("_WBsdt", tp, "$"), names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)
    mean2_col       <- grep(paste0("_WBmt", tp + 1, "$"), names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)
    sd2_col         <- grep(paste0("_WBsdt", tp + 1, "$"), names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)
    timelag_col     <- grep(paste0("_WBt", tp, "t", tp + 1, "$"), names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)
    correlation_col <- grep(paste0("_WBcort", tp, "t", tp + 1, "$"), names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)

    # Skip if any key column is missing
    if (length(mean1_col) != 1 || length(sd1_col) != 1 ||
        length(mean2_col) != 1 || length(sd2_col) != 1 ||
        length(timelag_col) != 1 || length(correlation_col) != 1) {
      next
    }

    # Create a new row for long format
    row <- static_info
    row$interval <- interval_label
    row[[mean1_col]] <- data[[mean1_col]][i]
    row[[sd1_col]] <- data[[sd1_col]][i]
    row[[mean2_col]] <- data[[mean2_col]][i]
    row[[sd2_col]] <- data[[sd2_col]][i]
    row[[timelag_col]] <- data[[timelag_col]][i]
    row[[correlation_col]] <- data[[correlation_col]][i]

    # Append the constructed row to long_rows list
    long_rows[[length(long_rows) + 1]] <- row
  }

  # Ensure all mean/sd columns are present in each row even if NA
  for (col in c(all_wbmt_cols, all_wbsdt_cols)) {
    if (!(col %in% names(row))) {
      row[[col]] <- NA
    }
  }
}

```

```{r Merging Output}
# Combine Long Format Data
long_format <- bind_rows(long_rows)     # Combine all reshaped rows into a single data frame
long_format[long_format == ""] <- NA    # Replace empty strings with NA

```


```{r Timepoints processing}
# Add Timepoints and Rename Original Column

long_format <- long_format %>%
  mutate(timepoints = as.numeric(`29_WbPoint`)) %>%  # Convert timepoints to numeric
  select(-`29_WbPoint`)                              # Rename original WB point column

```


```{r Filter Rows Based on Timepoints}

# Add interval start (e.g., for "1-2", interval_start = 1)
long_format$interval_start <- as.numeric(sub("-.*", "", long_format$interval))

# Retain only rows where interval start < max timepoints
long_format <- long_format %>%
  filter(is.na(timepoints) | interval_start < timepoints) %>%
  select(-interval_start)  # Drop temporary column

```


```{r Validation for accurate Output}
# Sanity Check on Key Columns
cols_to_check <- c("0_IDF", "1_ID", "1.1_CaseID", "2_Author", "3_Year", "29_WbPoint", "10_Size", "11_Mage", "16_CNT", "17_Eth", "18_Female","Negative/Positive+LS", "30_Covid", "31_WBm",  "33_Relt1", "34_Relt")

# Check if these columns exist in the data and print their types
for (col in cols_to_check) {
  if (col %in% names(long_format)) {
    cat(col, ":", class(long_format[[col]]), "\n")
  } else {
    cat(col, ": NOT FOUND in long_format\n")
  }
}

```

```{r Reorder columns manually by specifying the desired column sequence}

# Reorder columns manually by specifying the desired column sequence
long_format <- long_format %>%
  select(
      '0_IDF',                 # Unique study identifier
      '1_ID',                  # Study-level identifier
      '1.1_CaseID',            # Participant/case identifier
      '2_Author',              # Author of the study
      '3_Year',                # Year of publication
      'interval',              # Time interval label (e.g., "1-2")
      'timepoints',            # Total number of timepoints per case
      '10_Size',               # Sample size
      '11_Mage',               # Mean age
      '16_CNT',                # Country code
      '17_Eth',                # Ethnicity
      '18_Female',             # Percentage of females
      'Negative/Positive+LS',  # Affect type indicator: 0 = positive, 1 = negative
      '30_Covid',              # COVID-19 era study indicator
      '31_WBm',                # Global mean WB
      "33_Relt1",              # Reliability of T1 measurement
      "34_Relt",               # Reliability of T2 measurement

      # Repeated measures across sequential intervals: means, SDs, timelags, and correlations

      # Interval 1-2
      '38_WBmt1', '39_WBsdt1', '40_WBmt2', '41_WBsdt2', '45_WBt1t2', '46_WBcort1t2',

      # Interval 2-3
      '51_WBmt3', '52_WBsdt3', '48_WBt2t3', '49_WBcort2t3',

      # Interval 3-4
      '53_WBmt4', '54_WBsdt4', '58_WBt3t4', '59_WBcort3t4',

      # Interval 4-5
      '64_WBmt5', '65_WBsdt5', '61_WBt4t5', '62_WBcort4t5',

      # Interval 5-6
      '66_WBmt6', '67_WBsdt6', '71_WBt5t6', '72_WBcort5t6',

      # Interval 6-7
      '77_WBmt7', '78_WBsdt7', '74_WBt6t7', '75_WBcort6t7',

      # Interval 7-8
      '79_WBmt8', '80_WBsdt8', '84_WBt7t8', '85_WBcort7t8',

      # Interval 8-9
      '90_WBmt9', '91_WBsdt9', '87_WBt8t9', '88_WBcort8t9',

      # Interval 9-10
      '92_WBmt10', '93_WBsdt10', '97_WBt9t10', '98_WBcort9t10',

      # Interval 10-11
      '103_WBmt11', '104_WBsdt11', '100_WBt10t11', '101_WBcort10t11',

      # Interval 11-12
      '105_WBmt12', '106_WBsdt12', '110_WBt11t12', '111_WBcort11t12'
  )
```


```{r merge mean and SD as i and j for each Id}

# Extract and Merge Interval Columns


# Define relevant columns
static_cols <- c("1_ID", "1.1_CaseID", "2_Author", "3_Year", "interval", "timepoints", "10_Size","11_Mage", "16_CNT", "17_Eth", "18_Female","Negative/Positive+LS", "30_Covid", "31_WBm",  "33_Relt1", "34_Relt")


# Create list to store output rows
merged_rows <- list()

# Loop through rows in long_format
for (i in 1:nrow(long_format)) {
  row <- long_format[i, ]

  # Extract interval and parse i-j
  interval <- row$interval
  split <- str_split(interval, "-")[[1]]
  i_tp <- as.integer(split[1])
  j_tp <- as.integer(split[2])

  # Construct column names based on interval values
  WBmi_col <- grep(paste0("_WBmt", i_tp, "$"), names(row), value = TRUE)
  WBsdi_col <- grep(paste0("_WBsdt", i_tp, "$"), names(row), value = TRUE)
  WBmj_col <- grep(paste0("_WBmt", j_tp, "$"), names(row), value = TRUE)
  WBsdj_col <- grep(paste0("_WBsdt", j_tp, "$"), names(row), value = TRUE)
  WBcorr_col <- grep(paste0("_WBcort", i_tp, "t", j_tp, "$"), names(row), value = TRUE)
  WBt_col <- grep(paste0("_WBt", i_tp, "t", j_tp, "$"), names(row), value = TRUE)

  # Create compact row
  merged <- row[static_cols]
  merged$WBmi <- if (length(WBmi_col)) row[[WBmi_col]] else NA
  merged$WBsdi <- if (length(WBsdi_col)) row[[WBsdi_col]] else NA
  merged$WBmj <- if (length(WBmj_col)) row[[WBmj_col]] else NA
  merged$WBsdj <- if (length(WBsdj_col)) row[[WBsdj_col]] else NA
  merged$WBcorrij <- if (length(WBcorr_col)) row[[WBcorr_col]] else NA
  merged$WBtitj <- if (length(WBt_col)) row[[WBt_col]] else NA

  merged_rows[[length(merged_rows) + 1]] <- merged
}

# Combine all into final merged format
long_format_merged <- bind_rows(merged_rows)

# Preview the first few rows
head(long_format_merged)
```


```{r Merge the Long format columns}
# Step 1: Convert WBcorrij to numeric (if not already)
# This ensures that the WBcorrij column is treated as numeric for calculations (e.g., computing mean).
long_format_merged$WBcorrij <- as.numeric(long_format_merged$WBcorrij)

# Step 2: Compute mean of valid (non-NA and not equal to 9999) correlation values
# Remove rows with missing or placeholder (9999) values before calculating the mean correlation
valid_corrs <- long_format_merged$WBcorrij[!is.na(long_format_merged$WBcorrij) & long_format_merged$WBcorrij != 9999]

# Calculate the mean of the valid correlation values (ignoring any NA just in case)
mean_corr <- mean(valid_corrs, na.rm = TRUE)

# Step 3: Create a new dataframe and replace missing/invalid WBcorrij values with the mean
# Make a copy of the dataset to store cleaned correlation values
long_format_merged_corr <- long_format_merged

# Replace NA or 9999 in WBcorrij with the computed mean; keep the original value otherwise
long_format_merged_corr$WBcorrij <- ifelse(
  is.na(long_format_merged$WBcorrij) | long_format_merged$WBcorrij == 9999,
  mean_corr,  # Replace with mean
  long_format_merged$WBcorrij       # Keep original if valid
)

# Display the first few rows of the cleaned dataset for inspection
head(long_format_merged_corr)
```


```{r Computing Mean Change}
# Compute standardized mean change (dr)
long_format_merged_corr <- long_format_merged_corr %>%
  mutate(
    dr = ifelse(!is.na(WBmi) & !is.na(WBmj) & !is.na(WBsdi) & WBsdi != 0,
                (WBmj - WBmi) / WBsdi,
                NA_real_)
  )

# View the added Dr column
head(long_format_merged_corr)

# Save file with all the columns
write.xlsx(long_format_merged, "output/long_format_with_newcol.xlsx", rowNames = FALSE)
cat("Final long-format Excel written successfully with original column names intact.")

```

```{r Installing Packages for Meta Analysis}
# install metafor package
#install.packages(â€ťmetaforâ€ť)
#install.packages("matrix")

# install.packages("remotes")
#remotes::install_github("wviechtb/metafor")

# load metafor package
library(metafor)
```


```{r Meta-Analysis of Longitudinal Studies}

# Make sure your data is in the proper format
dat_meta <- long_format_merged_corr %>%
  rename(
    mean1 = WBmi,         # baseline mean
    mean2 = WBmj,         # follow-up mean
    sd1   = WBsdi,        # baseline SD
    n     =`10_Size`,     # sample size
    r     = WBcorrij,     # correlation  # r = WBcorrij
    study = `1_ID`        # unique ID for study/case
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    n = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(n))  # convert n into numeric for calculations
  )


# Calculate standardized mean change with raw score standardization
#dat_meta_escalc <- escalc(
#  measure = "SMCR",
#  m1i = mean2,      # follow-up mean
#  m2i = mean1,      # baseline mean
 # sd1i = sd1,       # baseline SD
#  ni = n,           # sample size
#  ri = r,           # correlation
##  data = dat_meta,
#  slab = study      # study label
#)

#head(dat_meta_escalc)
```

```{r Random Effect Model}
# Fit a random-effects meta-analysis model using the metafor::rma function
# yi = effect size estimates (e.g., standardized mean changes)
# vi = corresponding sampling variances for each effect size
# data = the dataset containing yi and vi (here: dat_meta_complete, assumed pre-cleaned)
res <- rma(yi, vi, data = dat_meta_complete)

# Display the summary results of the fitted random-effects model
# This includes the estimated overall effect size, confidence interval, heterogeneity statistics, etc.
res
```

```{r Random Effect Model Examining Result}
# fit random-effects model and examine results
# Filter out rows with missing yi or vi
dat_meta_complete <- dat_meta_escalc %>%
  filter(!is.na(yi) & !is.na(vi))

res <- rma(yi, vi, data=dat_meta_complete)
res
```

```{r updating plots view}

# Create forest and funnel plots side-by-side with better labeling
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))  # Layout for side-by-side plots

# Forest Plot
forest(res,
       xlab = "Standardized Mean Change (d)",         # X-axis label
       slab = dat_meta_complete$study,                 # Study labels
       mlab = "Pooled Effect (RE Model)",             # Summary label
       main = "Forest Plot of Effect Sizes")          # Main title

# Funnel Plot
funnel(res,
       xlab = "Standardized Mean Change (d)",         # X-axis
       ylab = "Standard Error",                       # Y-axis
       main = "Funnel Plot (Publication Bias)",     # Main title
       col = "black", pch = 19, bg = "lightgray")     # Aesthetic tweaks


```


```{r Lollipop graph}
# Load libraries
library(metafor)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
```


```{r Lollipop graph step 2}
res_df <- dat_meta_complete %>%
  mutate(
    # Compute standard error of the effect size (SEi = sqrt of variance)
    sei = sqrt(vi),
    
    # Compute Z-scores by standardizing effect sizes:
    # Subtract the mean and divide by standard deviation of yi
    z = (yi - mean(yi, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(yi, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  
  # Arrange rows in ascending order of Z-score (for plotting or inspection)
  arrange(z) %>%
  
  # Convert study column to a factor with levels ordered by appearance (preserves plot order)
  mutate(study = factor(study, levels = unique(study)))

```

```{r Lollipop graph step 2}
# Display the internal structure of the variable 'v_d' in the dataset 'dat_meta_complete'
# This shows type, length, and some example values
str(dat_meta_complete$v_d)

# Create a new data frame named 'res'
res <- data.frame(
  # Column 'study' copied directly from the original dataset
  study = dat_meta_complete$study,
  
  # Column 'yi' created from the effect size estimates (here, Cohen's d values)
  yi = dat_meta_complete$es_d,
  
  # Column 'sei' is the standard error of the effect size,
  # calculated as the square root of its variance 'v_d'
  sei = sqrt(dat_meta_complete$v_d)
)

```


```{r Plotting Lollipop graph}
# Step 3: Plot diverging lollipop chart
ggplot(res_df, aes(x = study, y = z, label = round(z, 2))) +   # Initialize ggplot with 'study' on x-axis, 'z' (z-scores) on y-axis, and text labels (rounded)
  geom_point(stat = 'identity', size = 4, color = "black") +   # Add black points for each study (the lollipop "head")
  geom_segment(aes(xend = study, y = 0, yend = z), color = "black") +   # Draw vertical line segments from 0 to the z-score (the lollipop "stick")
  geom_text(color = "white", size = 3, hjust = ifelse(res_df$z > 0, 1.2, -0.2)) +  # Add white text labels on each point, adjust text position depending on sign of z
  labs(
    title = "Diverging Lollipop Chart",                       # Main title of the chart
    subtitle = "Standardized Effect Sizes (Z-scores)",        # Subtitle
    y = "Z-score",                                            # Y-axis label
    x = NULL                                                  # Remove x-axis label (studies serve as labels)
  ) +
  coord_flip() +   # Flip coordinates to make it horizontal (studies on y-axis, z-scores along x-axis)
  theme_bw()       # Use a clean black-and-white theme
```


```{r}
facet_wrap(~ factor2, scales = "free_y")   # Split the plot into separate panels (facets) by the variable 'factor2'.
                                           # Each unique value of 'factor2' gets its own subplot.
                                           # 'scales = "free_y"' allows the y-axis to vary independently
                                           # in each facet, making labels and patterns clearer even if
                                           # groups have different numbers of studies or ranges of values.
```


```{r interactive plots}
# install.packages("meta")
library(meta)

# convert to meta format
meta_obj <- metagen(TE = yi, seTE = sqrt(vi), studlab = dat_meta_escalc$`1_ID`, data = dat_meta_escalc)

# interactive forest plot
meta::forest(meta_obj, interactive = TRUE)

```


```{r Removing some rows from data display}
# Drop rows with NA in required columns BEFORE fitting model and creating slab
dat_meta_escalc_clean <- dat_meta_escalc %>% 
  filter(!is.na(yi) & !is.na(vi))   # Keep only rows where effect size (yi) and variance (vi) are not missing

# Fit model
res <- rma(yi, vi, data = dat_meta_escalc_clean)   # Fit a random-effects meta-analysis model using metafor::rma()

# Now create slab of correct length
slab_labels <- paste0("ID", dat_meta_escalc_clean$`study`)   # Create study labels (slabs), one per study, prefixed with "ID"

# Plot
forest(res,                                    # Generate forest plot from the fitted model
       xlab = "Standardized Mean Change (d)",  # Label for x-axis
       slab = slab_labels,                     # Use custom study labels defined above
       order = order(dat_meta_escalc_clean$`X3_Year`),  # Sort studies in the plot by publication year
       mlab = "Pooled Effect (RE Model)",      # Label for pooled (meta-analytic) effect size
       main = "Forest Plot Sorted by Year",    # Main title of the forest plot
       cex = 0.6)                              # Reduce text size for better readability

```


```{r Forest Plot based on Year}
# Sort your dataset by year (for '3_Year' is the column)
dat_meta_escalc <- dat_meta_escalc[order(long_format_merged_corr$`3_Year`), ]

# Define colors by subgroup (timepoints)
subgroup_colors <- as.numeric(as.factor(long_format_merged_corr$timepoints))


# Refit the model (optional if already fitted)
res <- rma(yi, vi, data = dat_meta_escalc)

# Plot side by side
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

# Forest Plot: sorted, with subgroup labels
forest(res,
       xlab = "Standardized Mean Change (d)",
       slab = paste0(dat_meta_escalc$`1_ID`, " (", long_format_merged_corr$`3_Year`, ")"),
       order = order(long_format_merged_corr$`3_Year`),
       mlab = "Pooled Effect (RE Model)",
       main = "Forest Plot Sorted by Year")

# Add vertical line at 0
abline(v = 0, col = "gray60", lty = 2)

#  Funnel Plot: color-coded by timepoints
funnel(res,
       xlab = "Standardized Mean Change (d)",
       ylab = "Standard Error",
       main = "Funnel Plot by Timepoints",
       pch = 21, bg = subgroup_colors)

# Add vertical line at pooled estimate
abline(v = res$b, col = "blue", lwd = 2)
abline(v = 0, col = "gray60", lty = 2)

```

# Random mean level change similar to Narcisism
# res <- rma.mv(yi=es_dyear, V=v_dyear, random = ~ 1 | sampid/caseid, data=dat.agentic)

#yi=es_dyear: the effect size (standardized mean change per year).

#V=v_dyear: sampling variance of each effect size.

#random = ~ 1 | sampid/caseid: specifies random effects for nested structure:

#sampid = study ID (between-study variability),

#caseid = case/participant ID (within-study variability).

#data=dat.agentic: uses the agentic personality trait data.

#sum(dat.agentic.es_dyear.sample$n): total sample size across studies.



```{r split years in different chunks by 25 to see forest plot better}
library(metafor)

# Split into chunks of 25
n <- nrow(dat_meta_escalc_clean)
chunk_size <- 25
chunks <- split(1:n, ceiling(seq_along(1:n) / chunk_size))

# Loop through each chunk
for (i in seq_along(chunks)) {
  rows <- chunks[[i]]
  chunk_data <- dat_meta_escalc_clean[rows, ]  # Get the rows for this chunk

  # Fit rma using formula interface with proper subsetting
  res_chunk <- rma(yi ~ 1, vi = vi, data = chunk_data)

  # Create labels matching the length of the chunk
  slab_labels <- paste0(chunk_data$`1_ID`, " (", chunk_data$`X3_Year`, ")")

  # Plot forest
  forest(res_chunk,
         xlab = "Standardized Mean Change (d)",
         slab = slab_labels,
         main = paste("Forest Plot Chunk", i),
         mlab = "Pooled Effect (RE Model)",
         cex = 0.6)

  abline(v = 0, col = "gray60", lty = 2)
}

```


```{r Mean level change}
library(metafor)

library(dplyr)


# Step 1: Add row index to both data frames
dat_meta_escalc <- dat_meta_escalc %>%
  mutate(row_id = row_number())

long_format_merged_corr <- long_format_merged_corr %>%
  mutate(row_id = row_number())

# Step 2: Join 1.1_CaseID from long_format_merged_corr
dat_meta_escalc <- left_join(
  dat_meta_escalc,
  long_format_merged_corr %>% select(row_id, `1.1_CaseID`),
  by = "row_id"
)

# Step 3: Create required variables and clean NAs
dat_meta_escalc_clean <- dat_meta_escalc %>%
  mutate(
    es_dyear = yi / timepoints,             # standardized yearly effect size
    v_dyear  = vi / (timepoints^2),         # scaled variance
    sampid   = as.factor(study),
    caseid   = as.factor(`X1.1_CaseID`)
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(es_dyear) & !is.na(v_dyear) & !is.na(caseid) & !is.na(sampid)) %>%
  droplevels()

# Step 4: Run multilevel meta-analysis model
res_mean_change <- rma.mv(
  yi = es_dyear,
  V  = v_dyear,
  random = ~ 1 | caseid / sampid,
  data = dat_meta_escalc_clean,
  method = "REML"
)

# Step 5: Output results
print(res_mean_change, digits = 3)

# Optional: total sample size used
sum(dat_meta_escalc_clean$n, na.rm = TRUE)

```

# Robust Variance Estimation (by notes)

```{r Installing Robumeta Package}

#install.packages("robumeta")
library(robumeta)

```

# Now we need
# g: effect size (like yi or es_dyear)
# Var.g: corresponding sampling variance (like vi or v_dyear)
# IDCOUNTRY: study-level ID (i.e., study or 1_ID)


```{r Data Preparation}
# Prepare data (assuming you already calculated es_dyear and v_dyear)
dat_rve <- dat_meta_escalc %>%
  mutate(
    g = es_dyear,             # Effect size
    Var.g = v_dyear,          # Variance of effect size
    IDCOUNTRY = study         # Grouping variable (e.g., study ID)
  )

# View Data in Table format
head(dat_rve)
```
# What it means: 
# Rho = 0.5: assumed average correlation between effect sizes from the same study
# IÂ˛ = 97.07: percentage of variability due to heterogeneity (not sampling error)
# TauÂ˛ = 0.0027: between-study variance
# Estimate (Î˛â‚€ = -0.0177	): overall effect (intercept)
# t-value / p-value: -3.28: statistical significance
# CI (95%): confidence interval around the effect size
# by changing rho = 0.6, 0.8, etc., we can check sensitivity to within-study correlation assumptions.

```{r Run RVE Model }

# Checking  Representative Volume Element Model 
RVEce.study <- robu(
  formula = g ~ 1,                      # Intercept-only model
  data = dat_rve,
  studynum = IDCOUNTRY,                # Study-level clustering
  var.eff.size = Var.g,                # Variance of effect size
  small = FALSE,                       # No small-sample correction
  modelweights = "CORR",               # Correlated effects model
  rho = 0.5                            # Assumed correlation between outcomes
)
print(RVEce.study, digits = 3)


```


```{r Sensitivity Analysis}
# Perform sensitivity analysis on the fitted Robust Variance Estimation (RVE) model
# 'RVEce.study' is the meta-analysis model object created earlier using clubSandwich::robust()
# The sensitivity() function checks how sensitive the model results are to assumptions
# about within-study effect size dependence and small-sample corrections.
# This helps to assess robustness of conclusions against different modeling choices.
sensitivity(RVEce.study)
```


```{r Hierarchical Effects}

# Run RVE model assuming hierarchical (nested) structure
rve_hierarchical <- robu(
  formula = g ~ 1,                      # Effect size model (intercept-only)
  data = dat_rve,                       # Your dataset with effect sizes
  studynum = IDCOUNTRY,                 # Clustering variable (e.g., country ID)
  var.eff.size = Var.g,                 # Sampling variance of g
  small = FALSE,                        # Don't apply small sample correction
  modelweights = "HIER"                 # Hierarchical weights (nested)
)

# Print model summary
print(rve_hierarchical)

```

# Parameter: 	Meaning
# g ~ 1	Intercept-only model (no moderators)
# data = dat_rve dataset (must contain g, Var.g, IDCOUNTRY)
# studiynum	Tells robu() which grouping variable to use for clustering
# var.eff.size	Variance of each effect size (sampling variance)
# modelweights	"HIER" instructs it to assume a hierarchical model
# small = FALSE	Disables small-sample correction

# Output here:
# OmegaÂ˛: Between-cluster variance (e.g., differences between all studies)
# TauÂ˛: Within-cluster variance (e.g., variability across effect sizes per study)
# CI, t-values, p-values for the overall mean effect

```{r Random-effects model}
library(metafor)
library(dplyr)

# Step 1: Clean the data
dat_meta_escalc_clean <- dat_meta_escalc %>%
  filter(!is.na(yi) & !is.na(vi) & !is.na(study)) %>%  # Remove rows with NA in any required column
  mutate(study = as.factor(study)) %>%                # Convert study to factor (recommended)
  droplevels()

# Step 2: Fit the Random-Effects Model (REM)
REM <- rma.mv(
  yi = yi,
  V = vi,
  random = ~ 1 | study,         # Random intercept for each study
  data = dat_meta_escalc_clean
)

summary(REM)   # Show REM results

# Step 3: Fit the Fixed-Effects Model (FEM)
FEM <- rma.mv(
  yi = yi,
  V = vi,
  random = ~ 1 | study,         # Random structure still specified
  sigma2 = 0,                   # Force between-study variance to 0
  method = "REML",
  data = dat_meta_escalc_clean
)

summary(FEM)   # Show FEM results

# Step 4: Compare the models
anova(FEM, REM)


```
# REM: Estimates between-study variance (Ď„Â˛) â†’ tests for heterogeneity.
# FEM: Forces Ď„Â˛ = 0 â†’ assumes no heterogeneity.
# Comparing both models (e.g., via logLik, AIC, etc.) lets you assess whether heterogeneity exists.
# In summary(FEM) check sigma^2 = 0 and the test for heterogeneity: Q-test and its p-value.

# Log-Likelihood Values (LogLik)
#Full: -2426.36
#Reduced: -5116.72

#Interpretation: The Full model fits the data much better (higher log-likelihood) than the Reduced model.

#AIC / BIC / AICc
#Lower values of AIC, BIC, and AICc indicate better model fit.
#Full model: AIC = 4856.72
#Reduced model: AIC = 10235.43

# Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT / Ď‡Â˛)
# LRT = 5380.7150, p < .0001
# This tests whether the additional parameter(s) in the full model significantly improve the model fit.
#Since p-value < 0.0001 â†’ Full model is significantly better than the reduced model.

#df (Degrees of Freedom)
#Full model has 2 df, Reduced model has 1 df
#Likely, the Full model adds one more parameter (e.g., a random effect or variance component) over the Reduced model.



=======
---
title: " Calculate Raw Score and testing different model for meta analysis"
author: "Prerana Chandratre"
date: "02/07/2025"
output: html_document
---


```{r Load Required Libraries and Import Data}

# Load Required Libraries and Import Data
library(openxlsx)   # Library to read/write Excel files
library(dplyr)      # Library for efficient data manipulation using pipes and verbs
library(tidyr)      # Library for reshaping and tidying data
library(stringr)    # Library for string operations and pattern matching

# Define file paths
file_path <- "data/included_studies_1-13.xlsx"           # Path to the input Excel file (AgeWell dataset)
output_path <- "output/long_format_with_seq_corr.xlsx"   # Placeholder path for output (not used yet)

# Read the Excel file starting from row 2, assuming headers start on the second row
data <- read.xlsx(file_path, startRow = 2)

# Define a function to clean and standardize column names
clean_names <- function(x) {
  x <- gsub('["\']', "", x)          # Remove double and single quotes
  x <- gsub("&#10;?", "", x)         # Remove HTML newline codes (like '&#10;')
  x <- gsub("[\r\n]", "", x)         # Remove actual newline characters (carriage returns and linefeeds)
  x <- trimws(x)                     # Trim leading and trailing whitespace
  x <- gsub("\\s+", "", x)           # Remove all inner spaces
  return(x)                          # Return the cleaned name vector
}

# Apply the cleaning function to all column names of the data
names(data) <- clean_names(names(data))

```


```{r static-and-dynamic-columns}
# Define Static Columns and Filter WB Variables


# List of static columns (subject identifiers, authors, year, etc.)
keep_static <- c("0_IDF", "1_ID", "1.1_CaseID", "2_Author", "3_Year", "29_WbPoint", "10_Size", "11_Mage", "16_CNT", "17_Eth", "18_Female","Negative/Positive+LS", "30_Covid", "31_WBm", "33_Relt1", "34_Relt")

# Regex patterns to match dynamic WB columns
wb_patterns <- c(
  "^\\d+_WBmt\\d+$",           # e.g. 12_WBmt1
  "^\\d+_WBsdt\\d+$",          # e.g. 12_WBsdt1
  "^\\d+_WBt(\\d+)t(\\d+)$",   # e.g. 12_WBt1t2
  "^\\d+_WBcort(\\d+)t(\\d+)$" # e.g. 12_WBcort1t2
)

# Collect columns that match the WB patterns
wb_cols <- unique(unlist(lapply(wb_patterns, function(p) grep(p, names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE))))

# Filter to include only time-sequential intervals (e.g., 1-2, 2-3, ... 11-12)
is_sequential <- function(name) {
  match <- str_match(name, "t(\\d+)t(\\d+)")
  #if (any(is.na(match[1, 2:3]))) return(TRUE) 
  if (is.na(match[1,2]) || is.na(match[1,3])) return(TRUE)  # Allow mt/sdt columns without time ranges
  return(as.integer(match[1,3]) - as.integer(match[1,2]) == 1)  # Ensure intervals are sequential
  #return(as.integer(match[1, 3]) - as.integer(match[1, 2]) == 1)
}
wb_cols <- wb_cols[sapply(wb_cols, is_sequential)]

# Combine static and dynamic columns
all_cols <- intersect(c(keep_static, wb_cols), names(data))

# Subset the data with selected columns
data <- data[, all_cols, drop = FALSE]

# Convert all non-static (i.e., dynamic WB) columns to numeric
data[setdiff(names(data), keep_static)] <- lapply(data[setdiff(names(data), keep_static)], as.numeric)

```

```{r Adding time intervals}
# Define Time Intervals
timepoints <- 1:11                       # From timepoint 1 to 11 (for intervals 1-2 to 11-12)
intervals <- paste0(timepoints, "-", timepoints + 1)  # Create interval labels (e.g., "1-2", "2-3", etc.)

# Prepare an empty list to store reshaped rows
long_rows <- list()

```


```{r Transform Each Row into Long Format}

# Predefine expected WBmt and WBsdt column names
all_wbmt_cols <- paste0("_WBmt", 1:12)
all_wbsdt_cols <- paste0("_WBsdt", 1:12)

# Loop over each case/row
for (i in 1:nrow(data)) {
  static_info <- data[i, keep_static]  # Extract static info for this row

  # Iterate over each time interval (1-2, 2-3, ..., 11-12)
  for (tp in timepoints) {
    interval_label <- paste0(tp, "-", tp + 1)

    # Dynamically get column names for required variables for this interval
    mean1_col       <- grep(paste0("_WBmt", tp, "$"), names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)
    sd1_col         <- grep(paste0("_WBsdt", tp, "$"), names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)
    mean2_col       <- grep(paste0("_WBmt", tp + 1, "$"), names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)
    sd2_col         <- grep(paste0("_WBsdt", tp + 1, "$"), names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)
    timelag_col     <- grep(paste0("_WBt", tp, "t", tp + 1, "$"), names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)
    correlation_col <- grep(paste0("_WBcort", tp, "t", tp + 1, "$"), names(data), value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)

    # Skip if any key column is missing
    if (length(mean1_col) != 1 || length(sd1_col) != 1 ||
        length(mean2_col) != 1 || length(sd2_col) != 1 ||
        length(timelag_col) != 1 || length(correlation_col) != 1) {
      next
    }

    # Create a new row for long format
    row <- static_info
    row$interval <- interval_label
    row[[mean1_col]] <- data[[mean1_col]][i]
    row[[sd1_col]] <- data[[sd1_col]][i]
    row[[mean2_col]] <- data[[mean2_col]][i]
    row[[sd2_col]] <- data[[sd2_col]][i]
    row[[timelag_col]] <- data[[timelag_col]][i]
    row[[correlation_col]] <- data[[correlation_col]][i]

    # Append the constructed row to long_rows list
    long_rows[[length(long_rows) + 1]] <- row
  }

  # Ensure all mean/sd columns are present in each row even if NA
  for (col in c(all_wbmt_cols, all_wbsdt_cols)) {
    if (!(col %in% names(row))) {
      row[[col]] <- NA
    }
  }
}

```

```{r Merging Output}
# Combine Long Format Data
long_format <- bind_rows(long_rows)     # Combine all reshaped rows into a single data frame
long_format[long_format == ""] <- NA    # Replace empty strings with NA

```


```{r Timepoints processing}
# Add Timepoints and Rename Original Column

long_format <- long_format %>%
  mutate(timepoints = as.numeric(`29_WbPoint`)) %>%  # Convert timepoints to numeric
  select(-`29_WbPoint`)                              # Rename original WB point column

```


```{r Filter Rows Based on Timepoints}

# Add interval start (e.g., for "1-2", interval_start = 1)
long_format$interval_start <- as.numeric(sub("-.*", "", long_format$interval))

# Retain only rows where interval start < max timepoints
long_format <- long_format %>%
  filter(is.na(timepoints) | interval_start < timepoints) %>%
  select(-interval_start)  # Drop temporary column

```


```{r Validation for accurate Output}
# Sanity Check on Key Columns
cols_to_check <- c("0_IDF", "1_ID", "1.1_CaseID", "2_Author", "3_Year", "29_WbPoint", "10_Size", "11_Mage", "16_CNT", "17_Eth", "18_Female","Negative/Positive+LS", "30_Covid", "31_WBm",  "33_Relt1", "34_Relt")

# Check if these columns exist in the data and print their types
for (col in cols_to_check) {
  if (col %in% names(long_format)) {
    cat(col, ":", class(long_format[[col]]), "\n")
  } else {
    cat(col, ": NOT FOUND in long_format\n")
  }
}

```

```{r Reorder columns manually by specifying the desired column sequence}

# Reorder columns manually by specifying the desired column sequence
long_format <- long_format %>%
  select(
      '0_IDF',                 # Unique study identifier
      '1_ID',                  # Study-level identifier
      '1.1_CaseID',            # Participant/case identifier
      '2_Author',              # Author of the study
      '3_Year',                # Year of publication
      'interval',              # Time interval label (e.g., "1-2")
      'timepoints',            # Total number of timepoints per case
      '10_Size',               # Sample size
      '11_Mage',               # Mean age
      '16_CNT',                # Country code
      '17_Eth',                # Ethnicity
      '18_Female',             # Percentage of females
      'Negative/Positive+LS',  # Affect type indicator: 0 = positive, 1 = negative
      '30_Covid',              # COVID-19 era study indicator
      '31_WBm',                # Global mean WB
      "33_Relt1",              # Reliability of T1 measurement
      "34_Relt",               # Reliability of T2 measurement

      # Repeated measures across sequential intervals: means, SDs, timelags, and correlations

      # Interval 1-2
      '38_WBmt1', '39_WBsdt1', '40_WBmt2', '41_WBsdt2', '45_WBt1t2', '46_WBcort1t2',

      # Interval 2-3
      '51_WBmt3', '52_WBsdt3', '48_WBt2t3', '49_WBcort2t3',

      # Interval 3-4
      '53_WBmt4', '54_WBsdt4', '58_WBt3t4', '59_WBcort3t4',

      # Interval 4-5
      '64_WBmt5', '65_WBsdt5', '61_WBt4t5', '62_WBcort4t5',

      # Interval 5-6
      '66_WBmt6', '67_WBsdt6', '71_WBt5t6', '72_WBcort5t6',

      # Interval 6-7
      '77_WBmt7', '78_WBsdt7', '74_WBt6t7', '75_WBcort6t7',

      # Interval 7-8
      '79_WBmt8', '80_WBsdt8', '84_WBt7t8', '85_WBcort7t8',

      # Interval 8-9
      '90_WBmt9', '91_WBsdt9', '87_WBt8t9', '88_WBcort8t9',

      # Interval 9-10
      '92_WBmt10', '93_WBsdt10', '97_WBt9t10', '98_WBcort9t10',

      # Interval 10-11
      '103_WBmt11', '104_WBsdt11', '100_WBt10t11', '101_WBcort10t11',

      # Interval 11-12
      '105_WBmt12', '106_WBsdt12', '110_WBt11t12', '111_WBcort11t12'
  )
```


```{r merge mean and SD as i and j for each Id}

# Extract and Merge Interval Columns


# Define relevant columns
static_cols <- c("1_ID", "1.1_CaseID", "2_Author", "3_Year", "interval", "timepoints", "10_Size","11_Mage", "16_CNT", "17_Eth", "18_Female","Negative/Positive+LS", "30_Covid", "31_WBm",  "33_Relt1", "34_Relt")


# Create list to store output rows
merged_rows <- list()

# Loop through rows in long_format
for (i in 1:nrow(long_format)) {
  row <- long_format[i, ]

  # Extract interval and parse i-j
  interval <- row$interval
  split <- str_split(interval, "-")[[1]]
  i_tp <- as.integer(split[1])
  j_tp <- as.integer(split[2])

  # Construct column names based on interval values
  WBmi_col <- grep(paste0("_WBmt", i_tp, "$"), names(row), value = TRUE)
  WBsdi_col <- grep(paste0("_WBsdt", i_tp, "$"), names(row), value = TRUE)
  WBmj_col <- grep(paste0("_WBmt", j_tp, "$"), names(row), value = TRUE)
  WBsdj_col <- grep(paste0("_WBsdt", j_tp, "$"), names(row), value = TRUE)
  WBcorr_col <- grep(paste0("_WBcort", i_tp, "t", j_tp, "$"), names(row), value = TRUE)
  WBt_col <- grep(paste0("_WBt", i_tp, "t", j_tp, "$"), names(row), value = TRUE)

  # Create compact row
  merged <- row[static_cols]
  merged$WBmi <- if (length(WBmi_col)) row[[WBmi_col]] else NA
  merged$WBsdi <- if (length(WBsdi_col)) row[[WBsdi_col]] else NA
  merged$WBmj <- if (length(WBmj_col)) row[[WBmj_col]] else NA
  merged$WBsdj <- if (length(WBsdj_col)) row[[WBsdj_col]] else NA
  merged$WBcorrij <- if (length(WBcorr_col)) row[[WBcorr_col]] else NA
  merged$WBtitj <- if (length(WBt_col)) row[[WBt_col]] else NA

  merged_rows[[length(merged_rows) + 1]] <- merged
}

# Combine all into final merged format
long_format_merged <- bind_rows(merged_rows)

# Preview the first few rows
head(long_format_merged)
```


```{r Merge the Long format columns}
# Step 1: Convert WBcorrij to numeric (if not already)
# This ensures that the WBcorrij column is treated as numeric for calculations (e.g., computing mean).
long_format_merged$WBcorrij <- as.numeric(long_format_merged$WBcorrij)

# Step 2: Compute mean of valid (non-NA and not equal to 9999) correlation values
# Remove rows with missing or placeholder (9999) values before calculating the mean correlation
valid_corrs <- long_format_merged$WBcorrij[!is.na(long_format_merged$WBcorrij) & long_format_merged$WBcorrij != 9999]

# Calculate the mean of the valid correlation values (ignoring any NA just in case)
mean_corr <- mean(valid_corrs, na.rm = TRUE)

# Step 3: Create a new dataframe and replace missing/invalid WBcorrij values with the mean
# Make a copy of the dataset to store cleaned correlation values
long_format_merged_corr <- long_format_merged

# Replace NA or 9999 in WBcorrij with the computed mean; keep the original value otherwise
long_format_merged_corr$WBcorrij <- ifelse(
  is.na(long_format_merged$WBcorrij) | long_format_merged$WBcorrij == 9999,
  mean_corr,  # Replace with mean
  long_format_merged$WBcorrij       # Keep original if valid
)

# Display the first few rows of the cleaned dataset for inspection
head(long_format_merged_corr)
```


```{r Computing Mean Change}
# Compute standardized mean change (dr)
long_format_merged_corr <- long_format_merged_corr %>%
  mutate(
    dr = ifelse(!is.na(WBmi) & !is.na(WBmj) & !is.na(WBsdi) & WBsdi != 0,
                (WBmj - WBmi) / WBsdi,
                NA_real_)
  )

# View the added Dr column
head(long_format_merged_corr)

# Save file with all the columns
write.xlsx(long_format_merged, "output/long_format_with_newcol.xlsx", rowNames = FALSE)
cat("Final long-format Excel written successfully with original column names intact.")

```

```{r Installing Packages for Meta Analysis}
# install metafor package
#install.packages(â€ťmetaforâ€ť)
#install.packages("matrix")

# install.packages("remotes")
#remotes::install_github("wviechtb/metafor")

# load metafor package
library(metafor)
```


```{r Meta-Analysis of Longitudinal Studies}

# Make sure your data is in the proper format
dat_meta <- long_format_merged_corr %>%
  rename(
    mean1 = WBmi,         # baseline mean
    mean2 = WBmj,         # follow-up mean
    sd1   = WBsdi,        # baseline SD
    n     =`10_Size`,     # sample size
    r     = WBcorrij,     # correlation  # r = WBcorrij
    study = `1_ID`        # unique ID for study/case
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    n = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(n))  # convert n into numeric for calculations
  )


# Calculate standardized mean change with raw score standardization
#dat_meta_escalc <- escalc(
#  measure = "SMCR",
#  m1i = mean2,      # follow-up mean
#  m2i = mean1,      # baseline mean
 # sd1i = sd1,       # baseline SD
#  ni = n,           # sample size
#  ri = r,           # correlation
##  data = dat_meta,
#  slab = study      # study label
#)

#head(dat_meta_escalc)
```

```{r Random Effect Model}
# Fit a random-effects meta-analysis model using the metafor::rma function
# yi = effect size estimates (e.g., standardized mean changes)
# vi = corresponding sampling variances for each effect size
# data = the dataset containing yi and vi (here: dat_meta_complete, assumed pre-cleaned)
res <- rma(yi, vi, data = dat_meta_complete)

# Display the summary results of the fitted random-effects model
# This includes the estimated overall effect size, confidence interval, heterogeneity statistics, etc.
res
```

```{r Random Effect Model Examining Result}
# fit random-effects model and examine results
# Filter out rows with missing yi or vi
dat_meta_complete <- dat_meta_escalc %>%
  filter(!is.na(yi) & !is.na(vi))

res <- rma(yi, vi, data=dat_meta_complete)
res
```

```{r updating plots view}

# Create forest and funnel plots side-by-side with better labeling
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))  # Layout for side-by-side plots

# Forest Plot
forest(res,
       xlab = "Standardized Mean Change (d)",         # X-axis label
       slab = dat_meta_complete$study,                 # Study labels
       mlab = "Pooled Effect (RE Model)",             # Summary label
       main = "Forest Plot of Effect Sizes")          # Main title

# Funnel Plot
funnel(res,
       xlab = "Standardized Mean Change (d)",         # X-axis
       ylab = "Standard Error",                       # Y-axis
       main = "Funnel Plot (Publication Bias)",     # Main title
       col = "black", pch = 19, bg = "lightgray")     # Aesthetic tweaks


```


```{r Lollipop graph}
# Load libraries
library(metafor)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
```


```{r Lollipop graph step 2}
res_df <- dat_meta_complete %>%
  mutate(
    # Compute standard error of the effect size (SEi = sqrt of variance)
    sei = sqrt(vi),
    
    # Compute Z-scores by standardizing effect sizes:
    # Subtract the mean and divide by standard deviation of yi
    z = (yi - mean(yi, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(yi, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  
  # Arrange rows in ascending order of Z-score (for plotting or inspection)
  arrange(z) %>%
  
  # Convert study column to a factor with levels ordered by appearance (preserves plot order)
  mutate(study = factor(study, levels = unique(study)))

```

```{r Lollipop graph step 2}
# Display the internal structure of the variable 'v_d' in the dataset 'dat_meta_complete'
# This shows type, length, and some example values
str(dat_meta_complete$v_d)

# Create a new data frame named 'res'
res <- data.frame(
  # Column 'study' copied directly from the original dataset
  study = dat_meta_complete$study,
  
  # Column 'yi' created from the effect size estimates (here, Cohen's d values)
  yi = dat_meta_complete$es_d,
  
  # Column 'sei' is the standard error of the effect size,
  # calculated as the square root of its variance 'v_d'
  sei = sqrt(dat_meta_complete$v_d)
)

```


```{r Plotting Lollipop graph}
# Step 3: Plot diverging lollipop chart
ggplot(res_df, aes(x = study, y = z, label = round(z, 2))) +   # Initialize ggplot with 'study' on x-axis, 'z' (z-scores) on y-axis, and text labels (rounded)
  geom_point(stat = 'identity', size = 4, color = "black") +   # Add black points for each study (the lollipop "head")
  geom_segment(aes(xend = study, y = 0, yend = z), color = "black") +   # Draw vertical line segments from 0 to the z-score (the lollipop "stick")
  geom_text(color = "white", size = 3, hjust = ifelse(res_df$z > 0, 1.2, -0.2)) +  # Add white text labels on each point, adjust text position depending on sign of z
  labs(
    title = "Diverging Lollipop Chart",                       # Main title of the chart
    subtitle = "Standardized Effect Sizes (Z-scores)",        # Subtitle
    y = "Z-score",                                            # Y-axis label
    x = NULL                                                  # Remove x-axis label (studies serve as labels)
  ) +
  coord_flip() +   # Flip coordinates to make it horizontal (studies on y-axis, z-scores along x-axis)
  theme_bw()       # Use a clean black-and-white theme
```


```{r}
facet_wrap(~ factor2, scales = "free_y")   # Split the plot into separate panels (facets) by the variable 'factor2'.
                                           # Each unique value of 'factor2' gets its own subplot.
                                           # 'scales = "free_y"' allows the y-axis to vary independently
                                           # in each facet, making labels and patterns clearer even if
                                           # groups have different numbers of studies or ranges of values.
```


```{r interactive plots}
# install.packages("meta")
library(meta)

# convert to meta format
meta_obj <- metagen(TE = yi, seTE = sqrt(vi), studlab = dat_meta_escalc$`1_ID`, data = dat_meta_escalc)

# interactive forest plot
meta::forest(meta_obj, interactive = TRUE)

```


```{r Removing some rows from data display}
# Drop rows with NA in required columns BEFORE fitting model and creating slab
dat_meta_escalc_clean <- dat_meta_escalc %>% 
  filter(!is.na(yi) & !is.na(vi))   # Keep only rows where effect size (yi) and variance (vi) are not missing

# Fit model
res <- rma(yi, vi, data = dat_meta_escalc_clean)   # Fit a random-effects meta-analysis model using metafor::rma()

# Now create slab of correct length
slab_labels <- paste0("ID", dat_meta_escalc_clean$`study`)   # Create study labels (slabs), one per study, prefixed with "ID"

# Plot
forest(res,                                    # Generate forest plot from the fitted model
       xlab = "Standardized Mean Change (d)",  # Label for x-axis
       slab = slab_labels,                     # Use custom study labels defined above
       order = order(dat_meta_escalc_clean$`X3_Year`),  # Sort studies in the plot by publication year
       mlab = "Pooled Effect (RE Model)",      # Label for pooled (meta-analytic) effect size
       main = "Forest Plot Sorted by Year",    # Main title of the forest plot
       cex = 0.6)                              # Reduce text size for better readability

```


```{r Forest Plot based on Year}
# Sort your dataset by year (for '3_Year' is the column)
dat_meta_escalc <- dat_meta_escalc[order(long_format_merged_corr$`3_Year`), ]

# Define colors by subgroup (timepoints)
subgroup_colors <- as.numeric(as.factor(long_format_merged_corr$timepoints))


# Refit the model (optional if already fitted)
res <- rma(yi, vi, data = dat_meta_escalc)

# Plot side by side
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

# Forest Plot: sorted, with subgroup labels
forest(res,
       xlab = "Standardized Mean Change (d)",
       slab = paste0(dat_meta_escalc$`1_ID`, " (", long_format_merged_corr$`3_Year`, ")"),
       order = order(long_format_merged_corr$`3_Year`),
       mlab = "Pooled Effect (RE Model)",
       main = "Forest Plot Sorted by Year")

# Add vertical line at 0
abline(v = 0, col = "gray60", lty = 2)

#  Funnel Plot: color-coded by timepoints
funnel(res,
       xlab = "Standardized Mean Change (d)",
       ylab = "Standard Error",
       main = "Funnel Plot by Timepoints",
       pch = 21, bg = subgroup_colors)

# Add vertical line at pooled estimate
abline(v = res$b, col = "blue", lwd = 2)
abline(v = 0, col = "gray60", lty = 2)

```

# Random mean level change similar to Narcisism
# res <- rma.mv(yi=es_dyear, V=v_dyear, random = ~ 1 | sampid/caseid, data=dat.agentic)

#yi=es_dyear: the effect size (standardized mean change per year).

#V=v_dyear: sampling variance of each effect size.

#random = ~ 1 | sampid/caseid: specifies random effects for nested structure:

#sampid = study ID (between-study variability),

#caseid = case/participant ID (within-study variability).

#data=dat.agentic: uses the agentic personality trait data.

#sum(dat.agentic.es_dyear.sample$n): total sample size across studies.



```{r split years in different chunks by 25 to see forest plot better}
library(metafor)

# Split into chunks of 25
n <- nrow(dat_meta_escalc_clean)
chunk_size <- 25
chunks <- split(1:n, ceiling(seq_along(1:n) / chunk_size))

# Loop through each chunk
for (i in seq_along(chunks)) {
  rows <- chunks[[i]]
  chunk_data <- dat_meta_escalc_clean[rows, ]  # Get the rows for this chunk

  # Fit rma using formula interface with proper subsetting
  res_chunk <- rma(yi ~ 1, vi = vi, data = chunk_data)

  # Create labels matching the length of the chunk
  slab_labels <- paste0(chunk_data$`1_ID`, " (", chunk_data$`X3_Year`, ")")

  # Plot forest
  forest(res_chunk,
         xlab = "Standardized Mean Change (d)",
         slab = slab_labels,
         main = paste("Forest Plot Chunk", i),
         mlab = "Pooled Effect (RE Model)",
         cex = 0.6)

  abline(v = 0, col = "gray60", lty = 2)
}

```


```{r Mean level change}
library(metafor)

library(dplyr)


# Step 1: Add row index to both data frames
dat_meta_escalc <- dat_meta_escalc %>%
  mutate(row_id = row_number())

long_format_merged_corr <- long_format_merged_corr %>%
  mutate(row_id = row_number())

# Step 2: Join 1.1_CaseID from long_format_merged_corr
dat_meta_escalc <- left_join(
  dat_meta_escalc,
  long_format_merged_corr %>% select(row_id, `1.1_CaseID`),
  by = "row_id"
)

# Step 3: Create required variables and clean NAs
dat_meta_escalc_clean <- dat_meta_escalc %>%
  mutate(
    es_dyear = yi / timepoints,             # standardized yearly effect size
    v_dyear  = vi / (timepoints^2),         # scaled variance
    sampid   = as.factor(study),
    caseid   = as.factor(`X1.1_CaseID`)
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(es_dyear) & !is.na(v_dyear) & !is.na(caseid) & !is.na(sampid)) %>%
  droplevels()

# Step 4: Run multilevel meta-analysis model
res_mean_change <- rma.mv(
  yi = es_dyear,
  V  = v_dyear,
  random = ~ 1 | caseid / sampid,
  data = dat_meta_escalc_clean,
  method = "REML"
)

# Step 5: Output results
print(res_mean_change, digits = 3)

# Optional: total sample size used
sum(dat_meta_escalc_clean$n, na.rm = TRUE)

```

# Robust Variance Estimation (by notes)

```{r Installing Robumeta Package}

#install.packages("robumeta")
library(robumeta)

```

# Now we need
# g: effect size (like yi or es_dyear)
# Var.g: corresponding sampling variance (like vi or v_dyear)
# IDCOUNTRY: study-level ID (i.e., study or 1_ID)


```{r Data Preparation}
# Prepare data (assuming you already calculated es_dyear and v_dyear)
dat_rve <- dat_meta_escalc %>%
  mutate(
    g = es_dyear,             # Effect size
    Var.g = v_dyear,          # Variance of effect size
    IDCOUNTRY = study         # Grouping variable (e.g., study ID)
  )

# View Data in Table format
head(dat_rve)
```
# What it means: 
# Rho = 0.5: assumed average correlation between effect sizes from the same study
# IÂ˛ = 97.07: percentage of variability due to heterogeneity (not sampling error)
# TauÂ˛ = 0.0027: between-study variance
# Estimate (Î˛â‚€ = -0.0177	): overall effect (intercept)
# t-value / p-value: -3.28: statistical significance
# CI (95%): confidence interval around the effect size
# by changing rho = 0.6, 0.8, etc., we can check sensitivity to within-study correlation assumptions.

```{r Run RVE Model }

# Checking  Representative Volume Element Model 
RVEce.study <- robu(
  formula = g ~ 1,                      # Intercept-only model
  data = dat_rve,
  studynum = IDCOUNTRY,                # Study-level clustering
  var.eff.size = Var.g,                # Variance of effect size
  small = FALSE,                       # No small-sample correction
  modelweights = "CORR",               # Correlated effects model
  rho = 0.5                            # Assumed correlation between outcomes
)
print(RVEce.study, digits = 3)


```


```{r Sensitivity Analysis}
# Perform sensitivity analysis on the fitted Robust Variance Estimation (RVE) model
# 'RVEce.study' is the meta-analysis model object created earlier using clubSandwich::robust()
# The sensitivity() function checks how sensitive the model results are to assumptions
# about within-study effect size dependence and small-sample corrections.
# This helps to assess robustness of conclusions against different modeling choices.
sensitivity(RVEce.study)
```


```{r Hierarchical Effects}

# Run RVE model assuming hierarchical (nested) structure
rve_hierarchical <- robu(
  formula = g ~ 1,                      # Effect size model (intercept-only)
  data = dat_rve,                       # Your dataset with effect sizes
  studynum = IDCOUNTRY,                 # Clustering variable (e.g., country ID)
  var.eff.size = Var.g,                 # Sampling variance of g
  small = FALSE,                        # Don't apply small sample correction
  modelweights = "HIER"                 # Hierarchical weights (nested)
)

# Print model summary
print(rve_hierarchical)

```

# Parameter: 	Meaning
# g ~ 1	Intercept-only model (no moderators)
# data = dat_rve dataset (must contain g, Var.g, IDCOUNTRY)
# studiynum	Tells robu() which grouping variable to use for clustering
# var.eff.size	Variance of each effect size (sampling variance)
# modelweights	"HIER" instructs it to assume a hierarchical model
# small = FALSE	Disables small-sample correction

# Output here:
# OmegaÂ˛: Between-cluster variance (e.g., differences between all studies)
# TauÂ˛: Within-cluster variance (e.g., variability across effect sizes per study)
# CI, t-values, p-values for the overall mean effect

```{r Random-effects model}
library(metafor)
library(dplyr)

# Step 1: Clean the data
dat_meta_escalc_clean <- dat_meta_escalc %>%
  filter(!is.na(yi) & !is.na(vi) & !is.na(study)) %>%  # Remove rows with NA in any required column
  mutate(study = as.factor(study)) %>%                # Convert study to factor (recommended)
  droplevels()

# Step 2: Fit the Random-Effects Model (REM)
REM <- rma.mv(
  yi = yi,
  V = vi,
  random = ~ 1 | study,         # Random intercept for each study
  data = dat_meta_escalc_clean
)

summary(REM)   # Show REM results

# Step 3: Fit the Fixed-Effects Model (FEM)
FEM <- rma.mv(
  yi = yi,
  V = vi,
  random = ~ 1 | study,         # Random structure still specified
  sigma2 = 0,                   # Force between-study variance to 0
  method = "REML",
  data = dat_meta_escalc_clean
)

summary(FEM)   # Show FEM results

# Step 4: Compare the models
anova(FEM, REM)


```
# REM: Estimates between-study variance (Ď„Â˛) â†’ tests for heterogeneity.
# FEM: Forces Ď„Â˛ = 0 â†’ assumes no heterogeneity.
# Comparing both models (e.g., via logLik, AIC, etc.) lets you assess whether heterogeneity exists.
# In summary(FEM) check sigma^2 = 0 and the test for heterogeneity: Q-test and its p-value.

# Log-Likelihood Values (LogLik)
#Full: -2426.36
#Reduced: -5116.72

#Interpretation: The Full model fits the data much better (higher log-likelihood) than the Reduced model.

#AIC / BIC / AICc
#Lower values of AIC, BIC, and AICc indicate better model fit.
#Full model: AIC = 4856.72
#Reduced model: AIC = 10235.43

# Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT / Ď‡Â˛)
# LRT = 5380.7150, p < .0001
# This tests whether the additional parameter(s) in the full model significantly improve the model fit.
#Since p-value < 0.0001 â†’ Full model is significantly better than the reduced model.

#df (Degrees of Freedom)
#Full model has 2 df, Reduced model has 1 df
#Likely, the Full model adds one more parameter (e.g., a random effect or variance component) over the Reduced model.



>>>>>>> fb0254f3d5c6721e966e7c02d1c642553523eed8
